title: हस्तामलक स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री हस्तामलक स्तोत्रम् ॥

यो मां चक्षुः किमायातं चक्षुषो दृश्यमीक्षते।
दृश्यस्य किं चक्षुरथ ज्ञानमेकं स मे ह्यात्मा॥

योऽसौ सर्वजनप्रत्ययैकगम्यो मनोमयः।
जाग्रत्स्वप्नसुषुप्त्याद्यवस्थासु विभाव्यते।
सदा सच्चित्सुखात्मैकरूपः स मे ह्यात्मा॥ १॥

नेत्रस्य नेत्रं श्रवणस्य कर्णो
वाचां च वाचं मनसो हि मानम्।
प्राणस्य प्राणं स इहैव बुद्ध्वा
बुद्धो भवत्यात्मविदात्मनैव॥ २॥

यो भानुमानल्पमतिर्यथान्धो
घटे यथा भाति घटाम्बरं खम्।
तथा शरीरे स्फुरतीह बोधो
नित्यो विभुर्ब्रह्म स मे ह्यात्मा॥ ३॥

घटोदके यद्वदयं प्रतिबिम्बितेन्दुः
चलत्यसौ तद्वदयं शरीरे।
जीवो विवेकाज्जलवत्प्रसन्नो
निर्मुक्त एवात्मनि तिष्ठतीह॥ ४॥

मलीमसोपाधिवशाद्यथैन्दुः
भाति क्षणं शुद्ध इवापरः क्षणम्।
तथा सदात्मा गुणवत्प्रकाशते
विशुद्ध एवाविकृतः स्वभावतः॥ ५॥

यथा जलं शीतलमेव शुद्धं
दग्धं तपत्यम्बु तथाग्नियोगात्।
तथैव बुद्ध्यादिषु तप्तषु आत्मा
दग्ध इत्याहुरबोधतः खलु॥ ६॥

न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचित्
न वर्धते नापि च संक्षयं गतः।
अजो ध्रुवः शाश्वतमप्रमेयम्
मां विद्धि तं देहगतं परात्मानम्॥ ७॥

स्फटिके यथा लोहितता प्रतिभासते
जपाकुसुमसन्निधानतः।
तथा हि तेजो विशुद्धमात्मनः
शरीरसम्बन्धवशाद्भवत्ययम्॥ ८॥

एकः सदैवाद्वयमात्मतत्त्वं
न दृश्यवस्तून्यपि चान्यथा स्थितम्।
एकः स्वयंज्योतिरयं प्रकाशते
बुद्ध्यादि साक्षी परमात्मरूपः॥ ९॥

यो वै सर्वात्मभूतात्मा
सर्वभूतेषु संस्थितः।
सर्वाधारः स्वनिर्भासो
नित्यशुद्धः स मे ह्यात्मा॥ १०॥

प्रत्यगात्मा शिवोऽस्मीति
ज्ञानमात्रेण मुच्यते।
ज्ञानमात्रं परं ज्योति-
र्ब्रह्मैवाहमिति स्मरेत्॥ ११॥

गुरुं प्रणम्य श्रुतिवाक्यबोधो
यस्माद्भवेत्तत्त्वविनिश्चयोऽयम्।
हस्तामलक्यां प्रतिपादितोऽयम्
मुक्तिप्रदः सच्चिदानन्दरूपः॥ १२॥

॥ इति श्रीमच्छंकराचार्यविरचितम् हस्तामलक स्तोत्रम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Hastamalaka Stotram ||

Yo mam chakshuh kim-ayatam chakshusho drishyam-ikshate |
Drishyasya kim chakshur-atha jnanam-ekam sa me hy-atma ||

Yo-asau sarva-jana-pratyaya-eka-gamyo mano-mayah |
Jagrat-svapna-sushupti-ady-avasthasu vibhavyate |
Sada sach-chit-sukha-atma-eka-rupah sa me hy-atma || 1 ||

Netrasya netram shravanasya karno
Vacham cha vacham manaso hi manam |
Pranasya pranam sa iha-eva buddhva
Buddho bhavaty-atma-vid-atmanai-va || 2 ||

Yo bhanuman-alpa-matir-yatha-andho
Ghate yatha bhati ghata-ambaram kham |
Tatha sharire sphurati-iha bodho
Nityo vibhur-Brahma sa me hy-atma || 3 ||

Ghata-udake yad-vad-ayam pratibimbita-induh
Chalaty-asau tad-vad-ayam sharire |
Jivo vivekaj-jala-vat-prasanno
Nirmukta eva-atmani tishthati-iha || 4 ||

Malimasa-upadhi-vashad-yatha-induh
Bhati kshanam shuddha iva-aparah kshanam |
Tatha sada-atma guna-vat-prakashate
Vishuddha eva-avikritah svabhavtah || 5 ||

Yatha jalam shitalam-eva shuddham
Dagdham tapaty-ambu tatha-agni-yogat |
Tatha-eva buddhy-adishu taptashu atma
Dagdha ity-ahur-abodhatah khalu || 6 ||

Na jayate mriyate va kadachit
Na vardhate na-api cha samkshayam gatah |
Ajo dhruvah shashvatam-aprameyam
Mam viddhi tam deha-gatam paratmanam || 7 ||

Sphatike yatha lohitata pratibhasate
Japa-kusuma-sannidhantah |
Tatha hi tejo vishuddham-atmanah
Sharira-sambandha-vashad-bhavaty-ayam || 8 ||

Ekah sada-eva-advayam-atma-tattvam
Na drishya-vastuny-api cha-anyatha sthitam |
Ekah svayam-jyotir-ayam prakashate
Buddhy-adi sakshi Paramatma-rupah || 9 ||

Yo vai sarva-atma-bhuta-atma
Sarva-bhuteshu samsthitah |
Sarva-adharah sva-nirbhaso
Nitya-shuddhah sa me hy-atma || 10 ||

Pratyag-atma Shivo-asmi-iti
Jnana-matrena muchyate |
Jnana-matram param jyoti-
Brahmaiva-aham-iti smaret || 11 ||

Gurum pranamya shruti-vakya-bodho
Yasmad-bhavet-tattva-vinishchayo-ayam |
Hastamalaky-am pratipadito-ayam
Mukti-pradah sach-chid-ananda-rupah || 12 ||

|| Iti Shrimach-Shankaracharya-virachitam Hastamalaka Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
वह कौन है जो नेत्र से देखता है? नेत्र का दृश्य क्या है? एक ज्ञान ही — वही मेरा आत्मा है।

जो सब जनों की प्रतीति में एक रूप, मनोमय, जाग्रत-स्वप्न-सुषुप्ति अवस्थाओं में प्रतीत होता है — सदा सच्चित्सुख स्वरूप — वही मेरा आत्मा है॥ १॥

नेत्र का नेत्र, कान का कान, वाणी की वाणी, मन का मन, प्राण का प्राण — उसे यहीं जानकर बुद्ध (ज्ञानी) बनता है आत्मवित्॥ २॥

जैसे घड़े में आकाश भासता है, वैसे शरीर में बोध स्फुरित होता है — नित्य, विभु, ब्रह्म — वही मेरा आत्मा है॥ ३॥

जैसे घड़े के जल में प्रतिबिम्बित चन्द्रमा चलता प्रतीत होता है — विवेक से प्रसन्न जीव मुक्त होकर आत्मा में स्थित होता है॥ ४॥

जैसे स्फटिक लाल फूल के पास लाल दिखता है — वैसे शुद्ध आत्मा शरीर-सम्बन्ध से रंगा प्रतीत होता है॥ ८॥

एक, अद्वय, स्वयंज्योति, बुद्ध्यादि का साक्षी — वही परमात्मा रूप प्रकाशित होता है॥ ९॥

'मैं प्रत्यगात्मा शिव हूँ' — इस ज्ञान मात्र से मुक्ति होती है। 'ज्ञान ही परम ज्योति, ब्रह्म ही मैं हूँ' — ऐसा स्मरण करे॥ ११॥
benefits: Direct teaching of Atma-vidya in simple question-answer format
Spoken by young Hastamalaka to Adi Shankaracharya himself
Traditionally recited for removal of identification with body, mind, and senses
Reveals Atman as the eternal witness — Sat-Chit-Ananda
Invokes liberation through understanding one's true nature
Beautiful poetic examples make Vedanta accessible to all
description: Hastamalaka Stotram (हस्तामलक स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise dedicated to Lord Shiva. Drawn from Hastamalaka Stotram — Adi Shankaracharya, this text has been cherished by devotees for centuries. Devotees recite this prayer to direct teaching of Atma-vidya in simple question-answer format and spoken by young Hastamalaka to Adi Shankaracharya himself. It is also said to destroy identification with body, mind, and senses. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Monday (Somvar) and Thursday (Guruvar) and during Guru Purnima. At 12 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 8 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Hastamalaka Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Hastamalaka Stotram by Adi Shankaracharya in Sanskrit with transliteration and Hindi meaning. Twelve verses revealing the nature of Atman.
relatedSearches: Hastamalaka boy seven years old story

seoFaqs.question: Who actually composed the Hastamalaka Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The Hastamalaka Stotram was composed by Hastamalakacharya, a direct disciple of Adi Shankaracharya, not by Shankara himself. Tradition records that Hastamalaka was a young boy, said to be around seven years of age, when Shankara encountered him. In response to Shankara's question 'Who are you?', the child uttered twelve verses spontaneously expounding the nature of the Atman, which became known as the Hastamalakiyam. Adi Shankaracharya was so struck by the depth of these verses that he wrote his own commentary on them, addressing his disciple respectfully as 'Acharya'. The hymn therefore stands as a rare instance of a disciple's composition canonised by the master himself within the Advaita tradition.

seoFaqs.question: Why is this stotram called 'Hastamalaka'?
seoFaqs.answer: The name comes from the Sanskrit phrase hasta-amalaka, meaning 'an amalaka berry (Indian gooseberry) held in the hand'. In classical Sanskrit usage, holding an amalaka in one's palm is the standard metaphor for something that is perfectly clear, immediately visible, and beyond doubt. Adi Shankaracharya conferred this title on the disciple because his realisation of the Atman was as direct and self-evident to him as a fruit lying in his open hand. The stotram, in turn, takes its name from this epithet, signalling that its twelve verses present Self-knowledge with the same clarity, leaving no room for ambiguity about the witness-consciousness it describes.

seoFaqs.question: How many verses does the Hastamalaka Stotram contain and what do they teach?
seoFaqs.answer: The Hastamalaka Stotram consists of twelve concise Sanskrit verses. Each verse repeats the refrain identifying the speaker with the eternal, all-pervading Atman that is distinct from the body, senses, mind and intellect. The verses systematically negate identification with the five sheaths and assert the witness-consciousness as the true Self. Scholars consider the stotram a compact summary of Upanishadic teaching, which is why Shankaracharya himself wrote an elaborate bhashya on it. Devotees and Vedanta students chant it as a contemplative aid (manana), not as a petitionary prayer, since its purpose is to reorient identity toward pure awareness rather than to request worldly boons.

seoFaqs.question: Is the Hastamalaka Stotram considered a major Advaita Vedanta text?
seoFaqs.answer: Yes, within the Advaita corpus the Hastamalaka Stotram is grouped among the prakarana-style hymns that crystallise core Vedantic doctrine in devotional form. It is regularly anthologised alongside Shankara's own works such as Atma Bodha and Tattva Bodha because it carries Shankara's commentarial endorsement. The text is studied in traditional Vedanta pathshalas as an introduction to the doctrine of the witness-consciousness (sakshi-chaitanya) and the non-dual Self. Its brevity and clarity make it a recommended starting point for students before they tackle longer prakarana granthas, while its lineage as a disciple-composed, master-commented work gives it unique authority in the Shankara tradition.

seoFaqs.question: When and how is the Hastamalaka Stotram traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Unlike stotras tied to a specific deity festival, the Hastamalaka Stotram is recited primarily as a daily contemplative practice (nitya-parayana) by Advaita aspirants and monks of the Dashanami order. It is also chanted in Shankara mathas on Shankara Jayanti and on Guru Purnima as part of honouring the parampara. There is no fixed muhurta or elaborate ritual prescribed; the standard practice is to recite the twelve verses slowly with attention to meaning, often after morning sandhya or before scriptural study. Some teachers recommend pairing recitation with reflection on Shankara's commentary so that each verse functions as a pointer for self-enquiry rather than as mere chanting.

seoFaqs.question: Where can one find an authentic text and translation of the Hastamalaka Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Authentic Sanskrit editions with Devanagari, transliteration and translation are published by traditional Vedanta presses such as Chinmaya Publications and Advaita Ashrama (Ramakrishna Math), both of which include Shankara's commentary. The text is also reproduced on scholarly portals like Shlokam.org and Shankaracharya.org with verse-by-verse English meaning. For chanting, recordings released by Chinmaya Mission and Arsha Vidya Gurukulam follow the lineage's pronunciation. Devotees seeking a cross-checked version are advised to compare at least one print edition with a math-affiliated digital source, since many secondary websites omit verses or alter the order of the twelve shlokas.
